title: Epilogue To Lessingï؟½s Laocoï؟½n
content:
آ آ آ آ One Morn as through Hyde Park we walkï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ My friend and I, by chance we talkï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Of Lessingï؟½s famed Laocoï؟½n;
آ آ آ آ And after we awhile had gone
آ آ آ آ In Lessingï؟½s track, and tried to see
آ آ آ آ What painting is, what poetry,
آ آ آ آ Diverging to another thought,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Ah,ï؟½ cries my friend, ï؟½but who hath taught
آ آ آ آ Why music and the other arts
آ آ آ آ Oftener perform aright their parts
آ آ آ آ Than poetry? why she, than they,
آ آ آ آ Fewer real successes can display?

آ آ آ آ ï؟½For ï؟½tis so, surely! Even in Greece
آ آ آ آ Where best the poet framed his piece,
آ آ آ آ Even in that Phoebus-guarded ground
آ آ آ آ Pausanias on his travels found
آ آ آ آ Good poems, if he lookï؟½d, more rare
آ آ آ آ (Though many) than good statues were,
آ آ آ آ For these, in truth, were everywhere!
آ آ آ آ Of bards full many a stroke divine
آ آ آ آ In Danteï؟½s, Petrarchï؟½s, Tassoï؟½s line,
آ آ آ آ The land of Ariosto showï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ And yet, eï؟½en there, the canvas glowï؟½d
آ آ آ آ With triumphs, a yet ampler brood,
آ آ آ آ Of Raphael and his brotherhood.
آ آ آ آ And nobly perfect, in our day
آ آ آ آ Of haste, half-work, and disarray,
آ آ آ آ Profound yet touching, sweet yet strong,
آ آ آ آ Hath risen Goetheï؟½s, Wordsworthï؟½s song;
آ آ آ آ Yet even I (and none will bow
آ آ آ آ Deeper to these!) must needs allow,
آ آ آ آ They yield us not, to soothe our pains,
آ آ آ آ Such multitude of heavenly strains
آ آ آ آ As from the kings of sound are blown,
آ آ آ آ Mozart, Beethoven, Mendelssohn.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ While thus my friend discoursed, we pass
آ آ آ آ Out of the path, and take the grass.
آ آ آ آ The grass had still the green of May,
آ آ آ آ And still the unblackenï؟½d elms were gay;
آ آ آ آ The kine were resting in the shade,
آ آ آ آ The flies a summer murmur made;
آ آ آ آ Bright was the morn and south the air,
آ آ آ آ The soft-couchï؟½d cattle were as fair
آ آ آ آ As those that pastured by the sea,
آ آ آ آ That old-world morn, in Sicily,
آ آ آ آ When on the beach the Cyclops lay,
آ آ آ آ And Galatea from the bay
آ آ آ آ Mockï؟½d her poor lovelorn giantï؟½s lay.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Behold,ï؟½ I said, ï؟½the painterï؟½s sphere!
آ آ آ آ The limits of his art appear!
آ آ آ آ The passing group, the summer morn,
آ آ آ آ The grass, the elms, that blossomï؟½d thorn;
آ آ آ آ Those cattle couchï؟½d, or, as they rise,
آ آ آ آ Their shining flanks, their liquid eyes;
آ آ آ آ These, or much greater things, but caught
آ آ آ آ Like these, and in one aspect brought.
آ آ آ آ In outward semblance he must give
آ آ آ آ A momentï؟½s life of things that live;
آ آ آ آ Then let him choose his moment well,
آ آ آ آ With power divine its story tell!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Still we walkï؟½d on, in thoughtful mood,
آ آ آ آ And now upon the Bridge we stood.
آ آ آ آ Full of sweet breathings was the air,
آ آ آ آ Of sudden stirs and pauses fair;
آ آ آ آ Down oï؟½er the stately Bridge the breeze
آ آ آ آ Came rustling from the garden trees
آ آ آ آ And on the sparkling waters playï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Light-plashing waves an answer made,
آ آ آ آ And mimic boats their haven nearï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Beyond, the Abbey towers appearï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ By mist and chimneys unconfined,
آ آ آ آ Free to the sweep of light and wind;
آ آ آ آ While, through the earth-moorï؟½d nave below,
آ آ آ آ Another breath of wind doth blow,
آ آ آ آ Sound as of wandering breeze, but sound
آ آ آ آ In laws by human artists bound.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½The world of music!ï؟½ I exclaimï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½This breeze that rustles by, that famed
آ آ آ آ Abbey recall it! what a sphere,
آ آ آ آ Large and profound, bath genius here!
آ آ آ آ Thï؟½ inspired musician what a range,
آ آ آ آ What power of passion, wealth of change!
آ آ آ آ Some pulse of feeling he must choose
آ آ آ آ And its lockï؟½d fount of beauty use,
آ آ آ آ And through the stream of music tell
آ آ آ آ Its else unutterable spell;
آ آ آ آ To choose it rightly is his part,
آ آ آ آ And press into its inmost heart.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Miserere, Domine!
آ آ آ آ The words are utterï؟½d, and they flee.
آ آ آ آ Deep is their penitential moan,
آ آ آ آ Mighty their pathos, but ï؟½tis gone!
آ آ آ آ They have declared the spiritï؟½s sore
آ آ آ آ Sore load, and words can do no more.
آ آ آ آ Beethoven takes them then, those two
آ آ آ آ Poor, bounded words, and makes them new;
آ آ آ آ Infinite makes them, makes them young,
آ آ آ آ Transplants them to another tongue
آ آ آ آ Where they can now, without constraint,
آ آ آ آ Pour all the soul of their complaint,
آ آ آ آ And roll adown a channel large
آ آ آ آ The wealth divine they have in charge.
آ آ آ آ Page after page of music turn,
آ آ آ آ And still they live and still they burn,
آ آ آ آ Eternal, passion-fraught and free,
آ آ آ آ Miserere, Domine!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Onward we moved, and reachï؟½d the Ride
آ آ آ آ Where gaily flows the human tide.
آ آ آ آ Afar, in rest the cattle lay,
آ آ آ آ We heard, afar, faint music play;
آ آ آ آ But agitated, brisk, and near,
آ آ آ آ Men, with their stream of life, were here.
آ آ آ آ Some hang upon the rails, and some,
آ آ آ آ On foot, behind them, go and come.
آ آ آ آ This through the Ride upon his steed
آ آ آ آ Goes slowly by, and this at speed;
آ آ آ آ The young, the happy, and the fair,
آ آ آ آ The old, the sad, the worn were there;
آ آ آ آ Some vacant, and some musing went,
آ آ آ آ And some in talk and merriment.
آ آ آ آ Nods, smiles, and greetings, and farewells!
آ آ آ آ And now and then, perhaps, there swells
آ آ آ آ A sigh, a tear, but in the throng
آ آ آ آ All changes fast, and files along;
آ آ آ آ Hies, ah, from whence, what native ground?
آ آ آ آ And to what goal, what ending, bound?
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Behold at last the poetï؟½s sphere!
آ آ آ آ But who,ï؟½ I said, ï؟½suffices here?

آ آ آ آ ï؟½For, ah! so much he has to do!
آ آ آ آ Be painter and musician too!
آ آ آ آ The aspect of the moment show,
آ آ آ آ The feeling of the moment know!
آ آ آ آ The aspect not, I grant, express
آ آ آ آ Clear as the painterï؟½s art can dress,
آ آ آ آ The feeling not, I grant, explore
آ آ آ آ So deep as the musicianï؟½s lore,
آ آ آ آ But clear as words can make revealing,
آ آ آ آ And deep as words can follow feeling.
آ آ آ آ But, all, then comes his sorest spell
آ آ آ آ Of toil! he must lifeï؟½s movement tell!
آ آ آ آ The thread which binds it all in one,
آ آ آ آ And not its separate parts alone!
آ آ آ آ The movement he must tell of life,
آ آ آ آ Its pain and pleasure, rest and strife;
آ آ آ آ His eye must travel down, at full,
آ آ آ آ The long, unpausing spectacle;
آ آ آ آ With faithful unrelaxing force
آ آ آ آ Attend it from its primal source,
آ آ آ آ From change to change and year to year
آ آ آ آ Attend it of its mid career,
آ آ آ آ Attend it to the last repose
آ آ آ آ And solemn silence of its close.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½The cattle rising from the grass
آ آ آ آ His thought must follow where they pass;
آ آ آ آ The penitent with anguish bowï؟½d
آ آ آ آ His thought must follow through the crowd.
آ آ آ آ Yes, all this eddying, motley throng
آ آ آ آ That sparkles in the sun along.
آ آ آ آ Girl, statesman, merchant, soldier bold,
آ آ آ آ Master and servant, young and old,
آ آ آ آ Grave, gay, child, parent, husband, wife,
آ آ آ آ He follows home, and lives their life!

آ آ آ آ ï؟½And many, many are the souls
آ آ آ آ Lifeï؟½s movement fascinates, controls.
آ آ آ آ It draws them on, they cannot save
آ آ آ آ Their feet from its alluring wave;
آ آ آ آ They cannot leave it, they must go
آ آ آ آ With its unconquerable flow.
آ آ آ آ But, ah, how few of all that try
آ آ آ آ This mighty march, do aught but die!
آ آ آ آ For ill prepared for such a way,
آ آ آ آ Ill found in strength, in wits, are they!
آ آ آ آ They faint, they stagger to and fro,
آ آ آ آ And wandering from the stream they go;
آ آ آ آ In pain, in terror, in distress,
آ آ آ آ They see, all round, a wilderness.
آ آ آ آ Sometimes a momentary gleam
آ آ آ آ They catch of the mysterious stream;
آ آ آ آ Sometimes, a secondï؟½s space, their ear
آ آ آ آ The murmur of its waves doth hear.
آ آ آ آ That transient glimpse in song they say,
آ آ آ آ But not as painter can pourtray!
آ آ آ آ That transient sound in song they tell,
آ آ آ آ But not, as the musician, well!
آ آ آ آ And when at last these snatches cease,
آ آ آ آ And they are silent and at peace,
آ آ آ آ The stream of lifeï؟½s majestic whole
آ آ آ آ Hath neï؟½er been mirrorï؟½d on their soul.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Only a few the life-streamï؟½s shore
آ آ آ آ With safe unwandering feet explore,
آ آ آ آ Untired its movement bright attend,
آ آ آ آ Follow its windings to the end.
آ آ آ آ Then from its brimming waves their eye
آ آ آ آ Drinks up delighted ecstasy,
آ آ آ آ And its deep-toned, melodious voice,
آ آ آ آ For ever makes their ear rejoice.
آ آ آ آ They speak! the happiness divine
آ آ آ آ They feel, runs oï؟½er in every line.
آ آ آ آ Its spell is round them like a shower;
آ آ آ آ It gives them pathos, gives them power.
آ آ آ آ No painter yet hath such a way
آ آ آ آ Nor no musician made, as they;
آ آ آ آ And gatherï؟½d on immortal knolls
آ آ آ آ Such lovely flowers for cheering souls!
آ آ آ آ Beethoven, Raphael, cannot reach
آ آ آ آ The charm which Homer, Shakespeare, teach.
آ آ آ آ To these, to these, their thankful race
آ آ آ آ Gives, then, the first, the fairest place!
آ آ آ آ And brightest is their gloryï؟½s sheen
آ آ آ آ For greatest has their labour been.ï؟½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Matthew Arnold represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Matthew Arnold's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
